steps.title: Interpret Camillo's aside
steps.content: Camillo says he might ease the Sicilian king's sorrows, then adds that he may “o'erween,” or presume, to think so. The underlined portion therefore introduces doubt about his own claim.

steps.title: Reject overconfident readings
steps.content: Choice A overstates Camillo's concern as his primary motivation. Choice B shifts the focus to persuading Polixenes. Choice C wrongly makes the comfort belong to Polixenes rather than the king of Sicily.

steps.title: Name the speculative benefit
steps.content: The remaining choice matches Camillo's self-correction: his presence may benefit the king of Sicily, but he recognizes that this benefit is only speculative. The answer is D.
